texts.orig: Sparse PCA: Phase Transitions in the Critical Regime
texts.text: Sparse PCA: Phase Transitions in the Critical Regime

texts.label: text

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This work studies sparse principal component analysis (PCA) in high dimensions. Given n independent p -dimensional Gaussian samples with covariance Σ := ( λ -1) vv ⊤ + I p , our goal is to estimate v under the assumption of sparsity. On the one hand, if the sparsity level m := ∥ v ∥ 0 satisfies m ≲ √ n , algorithms such as covariance thresholding (Krauthgamer et al., 2015) consistently outperform PCA. On the other hand, if m ≫ √ n , it is conjectured that no polynomial-time algorithm can recover v below the detection threshold of PCA. We investigate the 'critical' high-dimensional regime, where n, p, m →∞ with m/ √ n → β and p/n → γ , and study estimators based on kernel PCA, generalizing covariance thresholding.
texts.text: This work studies sparse principal component analysis (PCA) in high dimensions. Given n independent p -dimensional Gaussian samples with covariance Σ := ( λ -1) vv ⊤ + I p , our goal is to estimate v under the assumption of sparsity. On the one hand, if the sparsity level m := ∥ v ∥ 0 satisfies m ≲ √ n , algorithms such as covariance thresholding (Krauthgamer et al., 2015) consistently outperform PCA. On the other hand, if m ≫ √ n , it is conjectured that no polynomial-time algorithm can recover v below the detection threshold of PCA. We investigate the 'critical' high-dimensional regime, where n, p, m →∞ with m/ √ n → β and p/n → γ , and study estimators based on kernel PCA, generalizing covariance thresholding.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Within this framework, we achieve a fine-grained understanding of signal detection and recovery. Our main result establishes a detection phase transition, analogous to the Baik-Ben Arous-P´ ech´ e (BBP) transition for PCA: above a signal strength threshold-depending on the kernel function, γ , and β -kernel PCA is informative. Conversely, below the threshold, kernel principal components are asymptotically orthogonal to the signal. Notably, (1) above this threshold, consistent support recovery is possible with high probability, (2) for all β ∈ (0 , ∞ ), kernel PCA strictly outperforms PCA, and (3) as β →∞ , kernel PCA and PCA coincide. We identify optimal kernel functions for detection and support recovery, and numerical calculations suggest that soft thresholding is nearly optimal. Our key technical contribution is approximation guarantees for deterministic equivalents of kernel random matrices, which enable sharp estimates of coordinate fluctuations of kernel principal components.

We identify optimal kernel functions for detection and support recovery, and numerical calculations suggest that soft thresholding is nearly optimal. Our key technical contribution is approximation guarantees for deterministic equivalents of kernel random matrices, which enable sharp estimates of coordinate fluctuations of kernel principal components.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From factor analysis to covariance estimation to matrix factorization, principal component analysis (PCA) is a standard tool for dimensionality reduction and low-rank signal recovery. Given data with n observations and p variables, the principal components are the eigenvectors of the p × p sample covariance matrix. PCA, however, has two key drawbacks: first, the principal components are generally non-sparse linear combinations of all p variables. This limits interpretability, and fails to capture the fact that signal matrices are often simultaneously low-rank and sparse-such matrices arise in diverse fields, including genetics, computer vision, imaging, and neuroscience [22, 23, 29, 55, 65]. Second, in high dimensions (when p is comparable to or greater than n ), PCA is inconsistent: the principal components are inconsistent estimators of the eigenvectors of the population covariance [57].

texts.orig: Remarkably, PCA experiences a phase transition : if λ ≤ 1 + √ γ , λ 1 converges to the upper edge of the support of the Marchenko-Pastur law, and u 1 is asymptotically orthogonal to v . On the other hand, if λ > 1+ √ γ , λ 1 is an outlier eigenvalue , occurring outside the Marchenko-Pastur support, and the cosine similarity between u 1 and v is non-trivial; that is, u 1 contains (partial) information about v . Key papers in the development of (1.2) and (1.3) include works of Baik, Ben Arous, and P´ ech´ e [6], Baik and Silverstein [7], and Paul [57], and the threshold 1 + √ γ is known as the Baik-Ben Arous-P´ ech´ e (BBP) transition. For a full list of references, see the survey [34].

texts.orig: When v is uniformly distributed on S p -1 , consistent detection (testing between λ = 1 and λ > 1) and non-trivial estimation are impossible for weak signal strengths below the BBP transition [58]. However, if v is sufficiently sparse, these key objectives become possible for such weak signals through alternative methods to PCA. In a seminal work, Johnstone and Lu [35] proposed the diagonal thresholding (DT) algorithm, which estimates the support of v by the indices of the largest-magnitude diagonal elements of Y . 1 Assuming knowledge of the sparsity level m := ∥ v ∥ 0 and that the entries of v satisfy v i ∈ { 0 , ± 1 / √ m ), Amini and Wainwright [3] proved that DT recovers the support of v with high probability if m ≤ C ( λ ) √ n/ log p .

texts.orig: This body of research raises the following question: is the condition m ≲ n necessary for computationally efficient estimation below the BBP transition? Ignoring computational considerations, a weaker condition suffices: by exhaustively searching over m × m principal submatrices of Y , exponential-time algorithms can recover the support of v if m log p ≲ n (for any fixed λ > 1), which is information-theoretically optimal [19, 66]. Conversely, work of Berthet and Rigollet [10] and ensuing papers strongly suggest that no polynomial-time algorithm can non-trivially estimate v below the BBP transition if m ≫ √ n . In summary, 'the sparse PCA problem demonstrates a fascinating interplay between computational and statistical barriers' [19].

texts.orig: A related body of work, including [59, 18, 47, 46, 58, 31, 51], considers estimation in the spiked model (1.1) where v 1 , . . . , v p are i.i.d. samples from a (fixed) prior distribution. By assuming a prior with a positive point mass on zero, this framework covers sparse PCA in the linear sparsity regime where m ≍ n . Deshpande and Montanari [18] and Perry et al. [58] considered a Bernoulli prior: v 1 , . . . , v p ∼ Ber( ρ ). While non-trivial estimation of v is impossible below the BBP transition, PCA becomes sub-optimal above the transition when ρ is sufficiently small. Specifically, there exists a critical value ρ c ≤ . 05, such that if ρ < ρ c , an efficient approximate message passing (AMP) algorithm is Bayes optimal [18]. For ρ > ρ c , PCA is conjectured to be optimal among polynomial-time algorithms [58].
texts.text: A related body of work, including [59, 18, 47, 46, 58, 31, 51], considers estimation in the spiked model (1.1) where v 1 , . . . , v p are i.i.d. samples from a (fixed) prior distribution. By assuming a prior with a positive point mass on zero, this framework covers sparse PCA in the linear sparsity regime where m ≍ n . Deshpande and Montanari [18] and Perry et al. [58] considered a Bernoulli prior: v 1 , . . . , v p ∼ Ber( ρ ). While non-trivial estimation of v is impossible below the BBP transition, PCA becomes sub-optimal above the transition when ρ is sufficiently small. Specifically, there exists a critical value ρ c ≤ . 05, such that if ρ < ρ c , an efficient approximate message passing (AMP) algorithm is Bayes optimal [18]. For ρ > ρ c , PCA is conjectured to be optimal among polynomial-time algorithms [58].

texts.orig: DT and CT are connected to the works of Bickel and Levina [11], Ma [49], and Cai, Ma, and Wu [15], all of which use thresholding to induce sparsity. In contrast to the spiked model, the first paper studies estimation of covariance matrices with L q quasi-norm sparse rows, where q ∈ [0 , 1). The third paper considers minimax estimation of the principal subspace of spiked models of the form Σ = V Λ V ⊤ + I p , where V has L q -sparse rows. They develop a data-driven algorithm based on DT which runs in polynomial time and achieves the minimax rate of convergence over a subset of the parameter space. Specializing their results to our model and L 0 -sparsity, their estimator is suboptimal by a logarithmic factor: it requires m ≤ C ( λ ) √ n/ log p to provably outperform PCA (see Theorem 7 and Proposition 1 of [15]).

[30], Feldman [27], Wang, Wu, and Fan [63], and Mergny et al. [50]. The first paper assumes delocalized (dense) spikes and investigates thresholding and quantization kernels (which reduce PCA's computational cost), finding that their application minimally impacts estimation. The second and third papers study a related model in which a transform f is applied elementwise to the data Z Σ 1 / 2 , rather than to the sample covariance Y .
